signals.message:
A struct field carries `time.Time` without a documented timezone marker
(no explicit UTC convention, no sibling string `tz` field, no method
annotating the field's location). When the producer and consumer run
in different timezones — or when a producer marshals "Local" time and
a consumer unmarshals it as wall-clock — aggregation windows shift,
audit logs misorder, and SQL `WHERE timestamp BETWEEN` queries silently
drop records. Either pin the field to UTC (a Validate-on-construct
step that calls `t.UTC()`), document the convention in the struct doc
comment, or store epoch nanoseconds.

a consumer unmarshals it as wall-clock — aggregation windows shift,
audit logs misorder, and SQL `WHERE timestamp BETWEEN` queries silently
drop records. Either pin the field to UTC (a Validate-on-construct
step that calls `t.UTC()`), document the convention in the struct doc
comment, or store epoch nanoseconds.

Code measures elapsed duration with `time.Since(...)` or
`time.Now().Sub(...)` against a wall-clock instant. On DST transitions,
manual clock adjustments, or NTP step events the returned Duration can
go negative or jump by hours, producing nonsense timings, fake SLA
breaches, or — when used as a loop guard — instant time-outs.
Prefer `time.Now()` paired with a monotonic-clock-safe helper, or
explicitly check `d < 0` and clamp.

Slice is preallocated with len(), filled by indexed assignment, and the
loop body can continue on error. That can leave zero-value holes in the
result; append to a zero-length slice when sparse output is not intended.

A per-element goroutine fanout is launched without an explicit
failure-independence barrier: each pump runs `go execPumpWriting(...)`
and the outer loop simply `wg.Wait()`s on completion. A single pump
that panics, hangs, or saturates a shared dependency (DNS resolver
cache, default HTTP transport pool, root logger) can corrupt
sibling-pump output or starve them. Document the independence claim
(process isolation, dedicated client, panic-recovery) AND wire a
cascade circuit-breaker per pump.

The purge cycle fans work out to N pumps via `go execPumpWriting(&wg, pmp, ...)`
and waits on a single `wg.Wait()`. The outer loop has no per-pump
success/fail accounting — partial-progress is invisible to operators,
health probes, or downstream alerting. When 7 of 8 pumps drop a
batch but the 8th succeeds, the purge cycle reports "done" with no
diagnosable signal. Expose per-pump result (records-written,
records-dropped, last-error) via a metric, log line, or job event.
Goroutine spawn requires lifecycle, cancellation, and shutdown ownership evidence.
Goroutine spawn lacks an obvious local lifecycle or cancellation cue.
Time-dependent behavior requires determinism, timeout, or scheduling evidence.
Goroutine spawn requires lifecycle, cancellation, and shutdown ownership evidence.

A goroutine spawned from a fanout (`go execPumpWriting(...)`,
`go func(){ ch <- pmp.WriteData(...) }()`) has no `defer
recover()` at the goroutine boundary. A panic inside any one pump's
WriteData (nil-pointer deref in a marshaller, divide-by-zero in
an aggregator) propagates up the goroutine stack, terminates the
process — taking sibling pumps and the purge loop with it.
The independence claim from `failure_independence_proven`
cannot hold without a per-fanout-goroutine recover barrier.

A TLS client config sets `InsecureSkipVerify: cfg.SSLInsecureSkipVerify`
from a config struct. The field is reachable from operator YAML
with no audit trail — a single misconfiguration disables certificate
validation across the deployment, MITM becomes trivially possible,
and there is no runtime warning. Either restrict the flag to an
explicit "development mode" that emits a startup WARN, or require a
paired `RootCAs` override so skipping verification AND ignoring the
system trust store cannot both happen by default.
Process-local filesystem API use requires path-boundary, lifetime, and error-handling evidence.

log.Fatal / os.Exit called from a non-main constructor or processing function.
This aborts the entire process and bypasses caller error handling; return
an error instead so the caller can recover.

A pump's `Init(config)` method calls `log.Fatal(...)` on a config
decode or connection failure. log.Fatal calls os.Exit(1), bypassing
every deferred cleanup AND every sibling pump's chance to keep
working. A single misconfigured pump in a multi-pump deployment
takes down the whole process. Either return the error to the caller
(the pump-init driver should decide whether the failure is fatal
for the WHOLE process, or just for THIS pump), or recover into a
degraded "this pump is inert" mode while others continue.

mapstructure.Decode silently ignores unknown keys in the input map. For
configuration parsing this can hide typos; use a DecoderConfig with
ErrorUnused: true when unknown keys should fail loudly.
Process-local filesystem API use requires path-boundary, lifetime, and error-handling evidence.

A creator that returns (handle, error) is called; the error is only
logged (no return / continue / fatal); execution then falls through to
a "defer outfile.Close()". When the creator fails the handle is nil and the
deferred Close panics with a nil-pointer dereference, masking the
original failure with a confusing stack trace. Return on the error arm
or guard the defer behind "if outfile != nil".

Function calls itself recursively after a sleep on the error arm with
no retry counter, max-attempt guard, or context-deadline check. Sustained
failure can grow the goroutine stack without bound.
Time-dependent behavior requires determinism, timeout, or scheduling evidence.

A method invokes itself recursively but the result is discarded — the
call is not prefixed with `return` and not captured into a variable.
Execution falls through to the original code path after the recursive
call completes, so any work performed by the inner call is duplicated
(the outer frame then runs the same loop a second time). Idiomatic
recursion uses `return r.MethodName(...)`.

Function receives a context.Context parameter but creates a fresh
context.Background() / context.TODO() inside the body and uses that
instead. Caller deadlines and cancellation can be silently dropped.

Code routes records into per-day SQL tables (`tyk_aggregated_YYYYMMDD`
shape) based on the wall-clock instant of the record. Two failure
modes: (a) the window boundary is interpreted half-open in one path
and closed in another, so a single record either lands in two shards
or none; (b) records arriving after a DST or clock-skew event get
routed to a stale shard, breaking range queries. The sharding
function must declare its boundary semantics (UTC-day, [start, end)
half-open) and a Validate step must enforce that the chosen instant
maps to exactly one shard.

A for loop uses a struct field as its stride: `for i := 0; i < N; i +=
cfg.BatchSize`, OR a shrink-loop uses a parameter as its slice step:
`for batchSize < len(s) { s = s[batchSize:] ... }`. When the field /
parameter is zero (default-constructed config, missing YAML key,
misconfigured caller) the loop hangs the goroutine forever and produces
no observable failure. Validate the field at init time and substitute a
non-zero default, OR guard the loop with `if cfg.BatchSize == 0 {
cfg.BatchSize = N }` immediately before the loop.

A method invokes itself recursively but the result is discarded — the
call is not prefixed with `return` and not captured into a variable.
Execution falls through to the original code path after the recursive
call completes, so any work performed by the inner call is duplicated
(the outer frame then runs the same loop a second time). Idiomatic
recursion uses `return r.MethodName(...)`.

Code wraps an operation with `backoff.RetryNotify(fn,
backoff.WithMaxRetries(backoff.NewExponentialBackOff(), N))`. The
max-retries count bounds the number of attempts but NOT the wall-clock
time spent — at default exponential growth (initial 500ms, multiplier
1.5, max-interval 1 minute) N=3 can take up to 90 seconds but N=10
can take over 15 minutes. A purge loop running at a 1-second interval
that retries for 15 minutes accumulates 900 missed purge slots; the
next successful purge is now flushing a saturated upstream. Bound the
retry budget with an absolute deadline (`backoff.WithContext(ctx,...)`,
`MaxElapsedTime`) so retries do not consume the whole interval.

can take over 15 minutes. A purge loop running at a 1-second interval

A TLS client config sets `InsecureSkipVerify: p.SSLInsecureSkipVerify`
from a config struct. The field is reachable from operator YAML
with no audit trail — a single misconfiguration disables certificate
validation across the deployment, MITM becomes trivially possible,
and there is no runtime warning. Either restrict the flag to an
explicit "development mode" that emits a startup WARN, or require a
paired `RootCAs` override so skipping verification AND ignoring the
system trust store cannot both happen by default.

A TLS client config sets `InsecureSkipVerify: p.hybridConfig.SSLInsecureSkipVerify`
from a config struct. The field is reachable from operator YAML
with no audit trail — a single misconfiguration disables certificate
validation across the deployment, MITM becomes trivially possible,
and there is no runtime warning. Either restrict the flag to an
explicit "development mode" that emits a startup WARN, or require a
paired `RootCAs` override so skipping verification AND ignoring the
system trust store cannot both happen by default.
Discarded call result requires explicit propagation or ignore-policy evidence.

A net.Conn returned by a custom dialer (closure-shaped, where conn IS
the success-return value) writes a setup payload in a loop. The
Write's error arm returns `(nil, err)` without first calling
`conn.Close()`. The connection (and its socket / FD / TLS session)
leaks. Add `defer conn.Close()` immediately after the successful dial,
OR an explicit Close() before the return-on-error in the loop.

leaks. Add `defer conn.Close()` immediately after the successful dial,
OR explicit Close() before the return-on-error in the loop.

A for loop uses a struct field as its stride: `for i := 0; i < N; i +=
cfg.BatchSize`, OR a shrink-loop uses a parameter as its slice step:
`for batchSize < len(s) { s = s[batchSize:] ... }`. When the field /
parameter is zero (default-constructed config, missing YAML key,
misconfigured caller) the loop hangs the goroutine forever and produces
no observable failure. Validate the field at init time and substitute a
non-zero default, OR guard the loop with `if cfg.BatchSize == 0 {
cfg.BatchSize = N }` immediately before the loop.
Time-dependent behavior requires determinism, timeout, or scheduling evidence.

log.Fatal / os.Exit called from a non-main constructor or processing function.
This aborts the entire process and bypasses caller error handling; return
an error instead so the caller can recover.

A pump's `Init(config)` method calls `log.Fatal(...)` on a config
decode or connection failure. log.Fatal calls os.Exit(1), bypassing
every deferred cleanup AND every sibling pump's chance to keep
working. A single misconfigured pump in a multi-pump deployment
takes down the whole process. Either return the error to the caller
(the pump-init driver should decide whether the failure is fatal
for the WHOLE process, or just for THIS pump), or recover into a
degraded "this pump is inert" mode while others continue.

configuration parsing this can hide typos; use a DecoderConfig with
ErrorUnused: true when unknown keys should fail loudly.

A for loop uses a struct field as its stride: `for i := 0; i < N; i +=
cfg.BatchSize`, OR a shrink-loop uses a parameter as its slice step:
`for batchSize < len(s) { s = s[batchSize:] ... }`. When the field /
parameter is zero (default-constructed config, missing YAML key,

no observable failure. Validate the field at init time and substitute a

cfg.BatchSize = N }` immediately before the loop.

Code routes records into per-day SQL tables (`tyk_aggregated_YYYYMMDD`
shape) based on the wall-clock instant of the record. Two failure
modes: (a) the window boundary is interpreted half-open in one path
and closed in another, so a single record either lands in two shards
or none; (b) records arriving after a DST or clock-skew event get
routed to a stale shard, breaking range queries. The sharding
function must declare its boundary semantics (UTC-day, [start, end)
half-open) and a Validate step must enforce that the chosen instant
maps to exactly one shard.

Library code registers handlers on or serves http.DefaultServeMux
(http.Handle / http.HandleFunc / http.ListenAndServe(..., nil)). The
DefaultServeMux is process-global; a second init in the same process
(re-init after config reload, parallel tests, two pumps configured for
different ports) panics with "http: multiple registrations for /metrics".
Construct an explicit *http.ServeMux and wire it through a private
*http.Server.

Goroutine spawn lacks an obvious local lifecycle or cancellation cue.

A TLS client config sets `InsecureSkipVerify: p.SSLInsecureSkipVerify`
from a config struct. The field is reachable from operator YAML
with no audit trail — a single misconfiguration disables certificate
validation across the deployment, MITM becomes trivially possible,
and there is no runtime warning. Either restrict the flag to an
explicit "development mode" that emits a startup WARN, or require a
paired `RootCAs` override so skipping verification AND ignoring the
system trust store cannot both happen by default.

A TLS client config sets `InsecureSkipVerify: p.config.SSLInsecureSkipVerify`
from a config struct. The field is reachable from operator YAML
with no audit trail — a single misconfiguration disables certificate
validation across the deployment, MITM becomes trivially possible,
and there is no runtime warning. Either restrict the flag to an
explicit "development mode" that emits a startup WARN, or require a
paired `RootCAs` override so skipping verification AND ignoring the
system trust store cannot both happen by default.

The body of `for k, v := range slice` reassigns `slice` itself via
`slice = append(slice[:k], slice[k+1:]...)` or via in-place compaction
(`copy(slice[k:], slice[k+1:]); slice = slice[:len(slice)-1]`). The Go
spec captures the range expression once at loop entry, so deleting
element k shifts element k+1 into position k while the loop counter
advances past it — every other matching element is skipped. Iterate
backwards, build a fresh result slice, or use slices.DeleteFunc.

Code measures elapsed duration with `time.Since(...)` or
`time.Now().Sub(...)` against a wall-clock instant. On DST transitions,
manual clock adjustments, or NTP step events the returned Duration can
go negative or jump by hours, producing nonsense timings, fake SLA
breaches, or — when used as a loop guard — instant time-outs.
Prefer `time.Now()` paired with a monotonic-clock-safe helper, or
explicitly check `d < 0` and clamp.

Code wraps an operation with `backoff.RetryNotify(fn,
backoff.WithMaxRetries(backoff.NewExponentialBackOff(), N))`. The
max-retries count bounds the number of attempts but NOT the wall-clock
time spent — at default exponential growth (initial 500ms, multiplier
1.5, max-interval 1 minute) N=3 can take up to 90 seconds but N=10
can take over 15 minutes. A purge loop running at a 1-second interval
that retries for 15 minutes accumulates 900 missed purge slots; the
next successful purge is now flushing a saturated upstream. Bound the
retry budget with an absolute deadline (`backoff.WithContext(ctx,...)`,
`MaxElapsedTime`) so retries do not consume the whole interval.

Code wraps an operation with `backoff.RetryNotify(fn,
backoff.WithMaxRetries(backoff.NewExponentialBackOff(), N))`. The
max-retries count bounds the number of attempts but NOT the wall-clock
time spent — at default exponential growth (initial 500ms, multiplier
1.5, max-interval 1 minute) N=3 can take up to 90 seconds but N=10
can take over 15 minutes. A purge loop running at a 1-second interval
that retries for 15 minutes accumulates 900 missed purge slots; the
next successful purge is now flushing a saturated upstream. Bound the
retry budget with an absolute deadline (`backoff.WithContext(ctx,...)`,
`MaxElapsedTime`) so retries do not consume the whole interval.

cenkalti/backoff ExponentialBackOff treats MaxElapsedTime = 0 as retry
forever. If unbounded retry is not intended, set a finite duration.
HTTP server API use requires listener, request-boundary, timeout, and static-file review evidence.

HTTP handler appears to return 200 unconditionally. If mounted as a
readiness probe, it may report ready before dependencies are reachable.

configuration parsing this can hide typos; use a DecoderConfig with
ErrorUnused: true when unknown keys should fail loudly.

Package-level mutable variable is assigned from a method body. Without
explicit synchronisation (sync.Mutex, atomic, or channel ownership) the
read/write pair is a data race when the method may be invoked from
multiple goroutines. Move the state behind a Mutex, use sync/atomic, or
scope it to a struct field with a documented owner.

Code calls `kv.Expire(ctx, key, expire)` on a Redis key whose lifetime
is governed by an absolute duration computed on the local clock. Two
failure modes: (a) the pump and Redis clocks disagree by ε, so the
pump's "this key is still alive" assumption races Redis's actual
eviction; (b) when the pump's clock jumps backwards (NTP step,
virtualization pause), the EXPIRE writes an expiration timestamp
that's already in the past, causing immediate eviction. Document
the clock-skew tolerance (`|pump.now - redis.now| < δ`) and either
assert it on init or switch to relative-TTL semantics validated by
a probe.
